PixiJS V5 Tutorial (2): What WebGL Actually Is, and How the Renderer Works
This post fills in some of the stuff I skipped in the previous one — things like what WebGL actually is, and a bit about the Renderer and the syntax around it.

I mentioned earlier that WebGL is a kind of JavaScript API. In short, WebGL lets you process images, pictures, and effects on the GPU and render them as part of a web Canvas. And right about now you might be going "okay, I know what a CPU is, but what's a GPU?"
CPU stands for Central Processing Unit. It's basically the heart of your computer.
So what about the GPU? GPU stands for Graphics Processing Unit — that's the graphics card you'll usually find inside your PC.
WebGL uses that graphics card to process Canvas imagery, which gives you a smoother experience. Without it, plain old Canvas can end up feeling a bit laggy.
So why did I drag us back to WebGL?
Mostly because earlier we used syntax like
. Renderer just means rendering, and the PixiJS Renderer has two ways of rendering things. Let's take a peek at the
The official comments cover a lot, so I'll just grab a small chunk and give it my best shot at translating.

This helper function will automatically detect which renderer you should be using.
WebGL is the preferred renderer as it is a lot faster. If WebGL is not supported by
the browser then this function will return a canvas renderer

Basically: this helper automatically picks which renderer you should use. WebGL is the first choice because it's faster, so you get a better experience. If the browser doesn't support WebGL, it falls back to native Canvas for rendering.
And here's the key bit of code:
@return {PIXI.Renderer|PIXI.CanvasRenderer} Returns WebGL renderer if available, otherwise CanvasRenderer
In the previous post we learned how to check the current version and which engine is being used — you just open the browser and look at the Console in DevTools.
Current version and engine in use
The other way is to do it in code:

This one fills in the gaps I skipped last time — what WebGL actually is, why PixiJS leans on your GPU, and how the Renderer works. I'll also show you two ways to check whether your browser is running on WebGL or falling back to plain Canvas.
